coredata.dc:title: One-pot dual product synthesis of hierarchical Co3O4@N-rGO for supercapacitors, N-GDs for label-free detection of metal ion and bio-imaging applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Chakradhar, Dasagrandhi
Karthik, Namachivayam
Perumal, Suguna
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Cobalt oxide nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (Co3O4@N-rGO) composite and nitrogen-doped graphene dots (N-GDs) were synthesized by a one-pot simple hydrothermal method. The average sizes of the synthesized bare cobalt oxide nanoparticles (Co3O4 NPs) and Co3O4 NPs in the Co3O4@N-rGO composite were around 22 and 24nm, respectively with an interlayer distance of 0.21nm, as calculated using the XRD patterns. The Co3O4@N-rGO electrode exhibits superior capacitive performance with a high capability of about 450Fg−1 at a current density of 1Ag−1 and has excellent cyclic stability, even after 1000 cycles of GCD at a current density of 4Ag−1. The obtained N-GDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe2+ and Fe3+, the limit of detection was as low as 1.1 and 1.0μM, respectively, representing high sensitivity to Fe2+ and Fe3+. Besides, the N-GDs was applied for bio-imaging. We found that N-GDs were suitable candidates for differential staining applications in yeast cells with good cell permeability and localization with negligible cytotoxicity. Hence, N-GDs may find dual utility as probes for the detection of cellular pools of metal ions (Fe3+/Fe2+) and also for early detection of opportunistic yeast infections in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt oxide nanoparticle
Nitrogen-doped carbon dot
Supercapacitor
Metal ion detection
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of bare Co3O4 NPs, GO, N-GDs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite
Determination of cytotoxicity of N-GDs in C. albicans
Structural characterization of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite
Electrochemical properties of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite
Surface and structural morphology of obtained N-GDs
Optical properties of the obtained N-GDs
Analytical sensing ability of obtained N-GDs
Mechanism for the label-free fluorescence quenching of N-GDs
Biological activities of the obtained N-GDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (grant number 2012M3A7B4049675 ) and the Priority Research Centers Program (grant number 2014R1A6A1031189 ). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of Co3O4@N-rGO composite and N-GDs.
(a) Wide angle XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra (c) FTIR spectra and (d) nitrogen physisorption isotherms of the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite.
XPS spectra of (a) survey scan, (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen, (d) oxygen and (e) cobalt of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite; (f) Pie chart of the chemical composition of Co3O4@N-rGO composite.
FESEM images with different magnification (a-c), and corresponding EDX elemental mapping images (d) cobalt (Co), (e) oxygen (O) and (f) EDX spectroscopy image of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs.

FESEM images with different magnification (a-d), and corresponding EDX elemental mapping images (e) carbon (C), (f) cobalt (Co), (g) oxygen (O), (h) nitrogen (N) and (i) EDX spectroscopy image of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite.
(a-e) HRTEM images with a different magnification of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite, and (f) corresponding SAED pattern of Co3O4 NPs.

FESEM image (a), and corresponding EDX elemental mapping images (b) carbon (C), (c) cobalt (Co), (d) oxygen (O), (e) nitrogen (N) and (f) EDX spectroscopy image of the working electrode (Co3O4@N-rGO composite coated on Ni-foam).

(a) CV measured with 5–200mVs−1 scan rates, (b) GCD measured at different current densities, (c) Cs change as a function of current densities 1–10Ag−1), (d) ED at different current densities, (e) PD at different current densities and (f) Cs retention (%) versus number of GCD cycles at a constant current density of 4Ag−1 of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 100mVs−1, (b) GCD curves at a current density of 1Ag−1 and (c) EIS spectra of the Nyquist plot for the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite; (d) is a suitably fitted circuit diagram of capacitance.
(a-c) HRTEM images with a different magnification of obtained N-GDs.
(a) excitation FL spectrum, (b) emission FL spectrum, (c) excitation-dependent emission spectra and (d) excitation dependent maximum emission wavelength of the obtained N-GDs.

(a) FL intensity at different metal ions, (b) metal ion affinity with ions concentration of 500μM, (c) FL intensity at different concentration of Fe2+, (d) selectivity investigation (F0-F)/F0 of Fe2+, (e) FL intensity at different concentration of Fe3+, and (f) selectivity investigation (F0-F)/F0 of Fe3+ on N-GDs.
Plausible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of obtained N-GDs in presence of Fe3+/ Fe2+ ions.
Confocal fluorescent microscopy images of C. albicans yeast after the uptake of N-GDs for 2 and 6h in bright field (BF), deep UV (248nm) and UV (408nm, blue) lasers filters.

(a) Time growth kinetics of C. albicans in the presence of N-GDs. Average ± SD growth of 2 independent experiments with duplicates was represented and (b) respiratory activity of C. albicans in the presence of different concentrations of N-GDs. The produced INF was quantified at 545nm. Sodium azide (NaN3) was used as positive control. Each experiment was repeated twice and average ± SD of four replicate value was represented.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: Characterization methods of Co3O4@N-rGO composite; Growth inhibition determination of N-GCDs; XRD Pattern, Raman spectrum, FT-IR spectrum and FESEM with elemental mapping of synthesized GO; Pore size distribution of the bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite; FESEM images of bare and modified 3D Ni-foam; Electrochemical study of bare Co3O4 NPs; Confocal fluorescent microscopy images of C. albicans under bright field, green and red filters, and C. neoformans yeasts under bright field, deep UV, blue, green and red filters after the uptake of N-GDs.

head.ce:title: One-pot dual product synthesis of hierarchical Co3O4@N-rGO for supercapacitors, N-GDs for label-free detection of metal ion and bio-imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Foodscience and Biotechnology, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Foodscience and Biotechnology, Kyungpook National University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt oxide nanoparticles@nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (Co3O4@N-rGO) composite and nitrogen-doped graphene dots (N-GDs) were synthesized by a one-pot simple hydrothermal method. The average sizes of the synthesized bare cobalt oxide nanoparticles (Co3O4 NPs) and Co3O4 NPs in the Co3O4@N-rGO composite were around 22 and 24nm, respectively with an interlayer distance of 0.21nm, as calculated using the XRD patterns. The Co3O4@N-rGO electrode exhibits superior capacitive performance with a high capability of about 450Fg−1 at a current density of 1Ag−1 and has excellent cyclic stability, even after 1000 cycles of GCD at a current density of 4Ag−1. The obtained N-GDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe2+ and Fe3+, the limit of detection was as low as 1.1 and 1.0μM, respectively, representing high sensitivity to Fe2+ and Fe3+. Besides, the N-GDs was applied for bio-imaging. We found that N-GDs were suitable candidates for differential staining applications in yeast cells with good cell permeability and localization with negligible cytotoxicity. Hence, N-GDs may find dual utility as probes for the detection of cellular pools of metal ions (Fe3+/Fe2+) and also for early detection of opportunistic yeast infections in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: High-performance supercapacitors owing to high specific capacitance are considered to be one of the most auspicious energy storage devices. Transition metal oxide nanoparticles are ideal pseudocapacitive materials due to their high theoretical capacitance obtained from the surface reversible redox reactions. Among various transition metal oxide nanoparticles, cobalt oxide nanoparticle has been suggested as a promising candidate for pseudocapacitors due to its excellent redox activity, cost-effectiveness and environment-friendly nature [1–5] . There are many varieties of cobalt oxide structures and compounds have been explored as electrode materials for supercapacitors, such as CoO and Co3O4. Among them, Co3O4 is the most familiar nanostructured material because of their excellent redox activity (reversibility) and high theoretical specific capacitance (3560Fg−1) [6–12]. Though, it is still some challenge to make a balance between excellent pseudocapacitive performance and simplicity of the synthetic pathway. Carbon related nanostructured materials have been used to improve the conductivity, energy and power density of the pseudocapacitive oxide materials. The Co3O4@N-rGO composite can improve the electrochemical performance, meet the demand for the practical application, facilitate the synergistic effect of two metal oxides, and as well as enhance the effect of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) on mechanical properties and conductivity to Co3O4. Several methods such as solvothermal, wet chemical, chemical vapour deposition, ultrasonic, microwave, hydrothermal and etc., have been most commonly used for the synthesis of Co3O4@graphitic carbon nanocomposite [13–18]. Among them, the hydrothermal method is a simple, direct and affordable strategy. Many researchers reported that the fabrication of Co3O4 decorated graphitic carbon nanosheets by hydrothermal route presents high capacity supercapacitors with excellent cycling stability [19–21].

On the other hand, carbon dots/graphene dots (CDs/GDs), a class of ‘zero-dimensional’ carbon nanomaterials, have extensive attention in recent years for a variety of potential applications in optoelectronic devices, sensors, biological labeling and bioimaging, due to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility as well as low toxicity and minimal photobleaching. A heteroatom such as sulfur and nitrogen functionalized GDs are wide and have a selective range of applications. Especially, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-GDs) have received much attention owing to their remarkable photoluminescence. Several common methods such as wet chemical, ultrasonic, hydrothermal, solvothermal and microwave to prepare GDs/N-GDs have been reported [22–27]. Of these, hydrothermal is one of the most common methods because of the simple, low-cost, eco-friendly and energy efficient. In general, the formation of CDs/GDs is generally under two classifications, top-down and bottom-up approaches. The CDs/GDs are generated from the bulk graphitic carbon materials known as top-down. The bottom-up approaches, CDs/GDs are prepared from carbonization of carbonaceous small molecules. The N-GDs possess both amino and carboxyl groups and are highly blue fluorescence (FL) and the relatively strong electron-withdrawing ability of N atoms in the N-GDs (high metal ion affinity) [28]. Thus, these N-GDs were served as a fluorescent probe for biological applications and label-free selective detection of metal ions. Among the various transition metal ions, iron is the most important metal plays a vital role in various biological processes including oxygen transport and exchange, and enzymatic reactions at the cellular level. Therefore, the detection of these transition metal (Fe3+/Fe2+) ions are highly coveted due to deepening concerns regarding industrial pollution. Various disorders such as anemia, Alzheimer's, and hemochromatosis are occurred due to the iron imbalance and can serve as a biological stimulus for commensal fungal pathogens such as Candida albicans (C. albicans) and Cryptococcus neoformans (C. neoformans) to become more virulent pathogens and cause secondary infections [29]. The application of fluorescent probes, observed using an FL microscope, can provide information on cellular pools of metal ions and their responses to changes in biological stimuli [30]. For the detection of biological iron many fluorophores have been reported, but their applications in living cells are limited, therefore further investigation of living cells fluorophores are essential. This investigation could be valuable to obtain new insight into the optical characteristics of the N-GDs and broaden to further explore N-GDs nanostructured materials with novel properties and promising applications [31].

The present work reports, one pot dual product synthesis of Co3O4@N-rGO composite and N-GDs (byproduct) using cobalt oxide nanoparticles (Co3O4 NPs), graphene oxide (GO) and aqueous ammonia (as a nitrogen source) by a simple hydrothermal method. The synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite was used as an electrode material for supercapacitors. The specific capacitance was evaluated using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS) in 2M KOH aqueous solution. The capacitive results of synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite were compared to the bare Co3O4 NPs and also to the earlier literature of carbon based Co3O4 NPs (Table S1). The water soluble byproduct (N-GDs) of Co3O4@N-rGO composite with bright FL were used as probes for the detection of metal ions in aqueous solution. In addition, the highly fluorescent N-GDs were evaluated as fluorescent probes for the selective staining of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells using bio-imaging in C. albicans and C. neoformans yeast pathogens by confocal laser scanning microscopy. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the optical performance of byproduct (N-GDs) has been utilized for the staining probe for FL bio-imaging and label-free detection of metal ion until now. Moreover, we reported the dual product with the multidisciplinary application by a one-pot process for the first time. This work is expected that the application of N-GDs as a sensing platform, can be extended to the environmental systems and bio-clinical applications in the near future.

Graphite powder (GP), N-methyl pyrrolidinone (NMP), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), carbon black (CB), ethanol and aqueous ammonia (25%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Ni-foam was purchased from the MTI Republic of Korea. The analytical grade of AlCl3, CaCl2, Cd(CH3OO)2, Co(OOCH3)2, CrCl3, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, HgCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, BaCl2, H2O2, H2SO4, HCl, KMnO4, tetrahydrofuran and acetone were purchased from Ducksan chemicals and were used as received. The yeast strains C. albicans and C. neoformans were procured from the Korean Cell Type Culture Collection (KCTC), Seoul, Republic of Korea, maintained in 10% v/v glycerol stocks, and stored at –80°C until further use. Overnight cultures in malt extract (ME) broth were used for the bio-imaging experiments. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bare Co3O4 NPs, GO, N-GDs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of bare Co3O4 NPs and pristine GO was adopted from our previously reported works [32,33]. The obtained bare Co3O4 NPs and pristine GO were used for the further synthesis of Co3O4@N-rGO composite. A typical synthesis procedure for the Co3O4@N-rGO composite is as follows, 30mg of GO was dispersed in 60mL of DI water into a Teflon cup with 100mL inner volume. The mixture was sonicated for 30min in order to disperse the GO layers. Subsequently, 90mg of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs were added to this mixture. Again, it was sonicated for 30min and followed by pH was adjusted to 11 by the addition of aqueous ammonia for the functionalization of the carbon materials with nitrogen. The Teflon cup slid into a stainless steel autoclave and was sealed carefully. Then, the final mixture was heated at 180°C for 12h in a hot air oven. After completion of the hydrothermal process, the resultant mixture was filtered through membrane filter paper (0.22µm). The black solid was collected and dried at 70°C for 12h in a hot air oven under static condition. The liquid was also collected and stored at 5°C until further use. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the synthetic route of Co3O4@N-rGO composite and N-GDs.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out in aqueous 2M KOH electrolyte with a three-electrode cell system with a saturated Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, platinum foil as a counter electrode and prepared material as a working electrode using a CorrTest-CS350 electrochemical workstation. The working electrodes were prepared using synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite, CB, and PVDF with wt% ratios of 80:10:10, respectively. Subsequently, the mixture was dispersed in NMP solution and sonicated for 15min in order to make a homogeneous slurry (paste). The slurry was coated onto a bare Ni-foam with an area of 1cm2 and was dried at 120°C in a hot air oven under static condition. The resulting modified Ni-foam (Co3O4@N-rGO composite) act as a working electrode for the electrochemical measurements. The electrochemical activity of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite were evaluated by CV and GCD techniques. The CV measurements of the modified Ni-foams (Co3O4@N-rGO composite on 3D Ni-foam electrode and bare Co3O4 NPs) were carried out in a potential window from −0.1 to 0.4V vs. Ag/AgCl at different scan rates (5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 and 200 mVs−1). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was conducted in a frequency range from 0.1Hz to 10kHz, under the perturbation of a sinusoidal wave of 5mV at the open circuit potential. The EIS data were fitted using Zview software. The GCD experiments were performed from 1 to 10Ag−1 in a potential window between 0 and 0.4V vs. Ag/AgCl. The specific capacitance (Cs) of the active material (Fg−1) was calculated from the GCD curves according to the following Eq. (1) [34,35].
(1)
C
s
=
I Δ t
m Δ V
where, I is the discharge current (A), ΔV is the potential difference (V), Δt is the discharge time variation (s), and m is the weight of the active material (g). The energy density (ED in Whkg−1) and power density (PD in kWkg−1) determines the operational efficiency and performance of electrochemical supercapacitors. The ED and PD were calculated from GCD using the Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively.
(2)
ED
=
0.5
C
s
( Δ V )
2
3.6
(3)
PD
=
ED 3600
Δ t
where Cs is the capacitance of the active material (Fg−1), ED is the energy density, ΔV is the potential change (V), and Δt is the discharge time (s).

The FL spectrum of a 1mL (0.5mL of stock solution + 0.5mL of DI water) suspension of obtained N-GDs was recorded and the maximum FL intensity was set as F0 (Blank). Then, the various metal ions such as Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ with a concentration of 500µM (0.5mL) were added into the suspension of N-GDs (0.5mL) and were gentle shaking under static condition in order to make homogeneity and complex formation. All the analytical experiments were carried out under the same conditions, and the corresponding FL spectra were recorded. The highly detected (highly quenched) metal ions were noticed and the detection experiments were repeated at different concentration (50–500µM as well as 10–50µM) in order to find the lower limit of detection (LOD).

Yeast strains such as C. albicans (KCTC-11282) and C. neoformans (KCTC-50554) were grown in ME broth (BD science, CA) at 28°C for overnight. The yeast cells (1 × 106 CFU/mL) were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) twice and re-suspended in PBS (pH 7.4) for the staining. The cell suspension was mixed with 10µL of N-GDs in distilled water and incubated at 28°C in stationary mode for cell culture. At designated time points, the cell suspension (1mL) was aseptically withdrawn and centrifuged at 6000rpm for 10min. The pellet was washed with PBS twice and re-suspended in 0.1mL of PBS. 10µL of the culture suspension was spotted onto an agarose pad, mounted on a microscopic glass slide. The FL imaging was performed using a Nikon Eclipse 80 I laser scanning confocal microscope. The FL was observed in deep UV, blue, green and red regions with excitation at 248, 408, 488 and 561nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cytotoxicity of N-GDs in C. albicans
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of the obtained N-GDs against the C. albicans yeast strain was evaluated by determining the redox activity of the C. albicans cells using iodonitrotetrazolium salt (INT). The typical procedure as follows: The C. albicans (5 × 106 CFU/mL) was prepared in 10mL of pre-filtered PBS, subsequently added with a concentration gradient of the test agent prepared in water. 50µL of 10µM INT solution was added to each solution and the glass tubes were incubated at 28°C for 6h in a dark place. 1mL of cell suspension was aseptically withdrawn from the glass tube and centrifuged to pellet the cells in every 60min interval. The cells were extracted with 0.5mL of DMSO and the extracted red INT-formazan product was spectrophotometrically measured at a wavelength of 545nm. Sodium azide with a concentration of 50µM was used as a positive control (PC). Each experiment was repeated twice and the mean ± SD value of respiratory activity was calculated. The percentage redox activity of cell/ cell viability (%) was determined as per the following Eq. (4):
(4)
C e l l
r e d o x
a c t i v i t y / c e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
A b
s
( S )
− A b
s
( B )
A b
s
( C )
− A b
s
( B )
× 100
where, Abs
(S)
, Abs
(B) , and Abs
(C) are the absorbance of the sample, blank, and control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite
ce:section.ce:para:
The powder XRD was used to investigate the phase and degree of crystallinity of the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite, which is shown in Fig. 2a. The XRD pattern of the bare Co3O4 NPs exhibited the dominant diffraction peaks at 19.1, 31.5, 37.0, 38.8, 45.0, 55.8, 59.5, 65.4 and 77.4°, which indexed to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (533) of spinel cobalt oxide, respectively and well match with the JCPDS Card No. 74–1657. Noticeably, the observed diffraction peaks in the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs are broadened, suggesting the smaller size of the resulting particles. The average size of the nanoparticles was calculated using the XRD pattern according to the line width of the (311) plane refraction peak by Scherrer Eq. (5). The XRD pattern of Co3O4@N-rGO composite exhibit a broad peak around 25° are explicitly indexed to C(002) diffractions plane of the hexagonal graphite structure, additional with planes of Co3O4 NPs [36–38]. The peak intensity of C(002) plane is very small compared to Co3O4 peaks, this can be accredited to the fact that the amount of Co3O4 NPs is relatively large, which reduces the diffraction intensity of rGO. The d-spacing value of about 0.35nm was calculated based on the lattice plane C(002) using the Bragg Eq. (6), which is larger than that of bulk graphite (∼0.34nm), perhaps due to the nitrogen-containing groups introduced in the synthesis process [39]. Moreover, the GO completely converted into rGO during the synthesis of Co3O4@N-rGO composite (see the XRD pattern of GO, Fig. S1a).
(5)
L =
K λ
β
cos
θ
where L is the average size of the crystallites (nanoparticles), K is a dimensionless shape factor (0.9), λ is the wavelength of incident X-ray (λ = 1.54Å), β is the full width at half maximum of the diffraction peak and θ refer to the position of the (311) peak (the angle between the incident rays and the scattering planes).
(6)
n λ = 2 d s i n θ
(
o r
)
d =
n λ
2
sin
θ
where n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray (λ = 1.54Å) and θ is the angle between the incident rays and the scattering planes (the surface of the analyzing materials).

Raman spectra are one of the key proofs to demonstrate the presence of rGO and degree of graphitization in the Co3O4@N-rGO composite. The Raman spectra of the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite are shown in Fig. 2b. The spectrum of Co3O4@N-rGO composite shows two predominant Raman peak at about 1350 and 1590cm−1, denoted as D and G-bands, respectively [40–43]. The D and G-bands originated from defected graphene layers (structure disorder) and the sp2-hybridized carbon in a 2-dimensional hexagonal lattice, respectively. Other than these two peaks, the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite display the stretching vibration peaks below 700cm−1. These peaks correspond to F2g, Eg and A1g mode of the crystalline Co3O4 phase. The I
D
/I
G value was about 1.1 and the intensity ratio indicates that the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite had a moderate graphitic nature. The I
D
/I
G value was about 1.2 for pristine GO (see the ESI Fig. S1b). The intensity ratio of D to G-band indicates that the GO has been reduced in Co3O4@rGO composite and these results strongly suggest the Co3O4@N-rGO composite has been successfully synthesized without any contamination [44].

The FTIR spectroscopy has been examined to confirm the existence of cubic spinel structure of Co3O4 NPs. Fig. 2c shows the FTIR spectra of the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite. As shown in FTIR spectrum of bare Co3O4 NPs, the two prominent IR bands at 537 and 654cm−1 which ascribe to Co–O vibration mode and the bridging vibration of O–Co–O [45,46]. By comparison, the spectrum of Co3O4@N-rGO composite shows similar as that of bare Co3O4 NPs with additional graphene functional groups. Apart from Co3O4 NPs peaks, Co3O4@N-rGO composite shows the absorption band of the aromatic C˭C stretching vibration at 1560cm−1 indicates the presence of a sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice from the rGO. The absorption bands at 1455cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of aromatic C–N heterocycles were merged with C˭C and showed a broad band [47]. In addition, there is no prominent N–H in-plane stretching of an amine group was observed in the FTIR spectrum of synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite but only the C–N vibration peak was appeared, suggesting the nitrogen atoms were successfully incorporated into the rGO. The absorption band at 1210cm−1 was consigned to the stretching vibration of C–O groups. The stretching vibrations of epoxy C–O–C and C–N–C groups were merged and exhibited by the peaks at 1000cm−1 which reveal the nitrogen moiety functionalized with the resulting materials [48]. The –OH and –C˭O groups of GO, nearly disappeared for the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite which confirms the GO converted to rGO during the hydrothermal synthesis (see the ESI Fig. S1c). In addition, the absorption band of C˭C and C–O–C functional groups quite broadening due to functionalization of nitrogen groups. The shape and peak positions of the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite is fairly different from that of GO, implying the corresponding change in the coordination environment of various functional groups in the GO layer after the reaction and reduced to rGO [49].

The surface area of the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite was examined by N2 sorption isotherms as shown in Fig. 2d. The physisorption isotherms of Co3O4@N-rGO composite indicate the presence of mesopores with a continuous size distribution. The synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite exhibiting a type IV isotherm with H3-type hysteresis loop, the sharp capillary condensation step at high relative pressures (P/P
0 ) > 0.9, indicating the existence of large pores in the material [50]. Fig. S1d shows the pore size distribution of the bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite. The Co3O4@N-rGO composite possesses single pore size is around 4nm but the bare Co3O4 NPs does not have a significant pore size which is clear from the magnified pore size distribution graphs (inset Fig. S1d). The specific surface area of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite is nearly 67m2 g−1 with a pore size and pore volume of approximately 4nm and 0.5cm3 g−1, respectively. The surface area of Co3O4@N-rGO composite was nearly 20 times higher than bare Co3O4 NPs. The BET surface area results concluding that adequate space is available in Co3O4@N-rGO composite for moving electrolyte one side to another side without struggling. The synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite has a high surface area as well as high degree of graphitization. The high surface area with large pore size, pore volume and a high degree of graphitization exhibited nanocomposite is believed to be beneficial for the diffusion of the electrolyte to the active sites of the electrode and will make a positive contribution to its electrochemical performance [51].

The chemical compositions of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite were revealed by XPS analysis and were presented in Fig. 3. Peaks corresponding to carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and cobalt are detected in the survey scan spectrum, indicating that only C, N, O and Co elements are clearly observed in the Co3O4@N-rGO composite (Fig. 3a). Further, the high-resolution spectra were used to estimate the functional groups and binding atoms within C1s, N1s, O1s and Co2p levels. The deconvoluted C1s level in Fig. 3b shows a slightly asymmetric tail at higher binding energy, which is a common characteristic of nitrogen-doped carbon materials. The peaks at 284.4 and 284.6eV are assigned to non-oxygenated carbon atoms such as sp2-hybridized graphite-like carbon (C˭C) and sp3-hybridized diamond-like carbon (C–C), respectively [52]. The predominant peak at 284.4eV clearly indicated that the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite exhibited major sp2-hybridized graphitic carbons. The peaks positioned at 285.5 and 287.3eV are ascribed to C–O/C–N and C˭O/C˭N groups, respectively which are originated from the surface oxygen and nitrogen groups. Other than these four peaks, an additional peak observed at 291.4eV indicates the shake-up satellites of sp2-hybridized graphite-like carbon which is due to carbonization and aromatization during the prolonged hydrothermal reduction of rGO [53]. Additionally, the absence of peaks at ∼282eV in the deconvoluted C1s level for the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite, which revealed that no carbon is chemically bonded with the lattice of Co3O4 NPs (Co–C/Co-O-C bond). As a consequence, it is proved that the carbon species incorporated in Co3O4 NPs which is further supported by the FTIR spectroscopy results [54].

Fig. 3c shows the deconvoluted high-resolution spectrum of N1s region exhibited three peaks at 398.4, 399.6 and 401eV are attributed to pyridinic-N (C–N), pyrrolic-N (N–H) and graphitic-N ((C)3–N) groups, respectively [55]. Fig. 3d presents the high-resolution spectrum of O1s region, and the deconvoluted peaks at 529.7, 531.0, 532.4 and 534.1eV could be observed, corresponding to the lattice oxygen in the spinel Co3O4 (O–Co), oxygen of the hydroxide ions (Co–OH), the oxygen in C˭O/C–O bonds of epoxy/alkoxy and the water (H–O–H) adsorbed onto the surface of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite, respectively [56,57]. The high-resolution spectrum of Co2p (Fig. 3e) exhibits two predominant peaks located at 781.3 and 797.5eV corresponding to the electronic states of Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2, respectively. The gap between the Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 peaks is about 16.2eV, which is higher than that of a typical characteristic of the standard Co3O4 spectra [58]. The presence of Co3O4 NPs can be further confirmed by the O1s peak located at 529.7 and 531.0eV, which corresponds to the oxygen species with cobalt elements. The shoulder peaks at 786.7 and 804.7eV are credited to the shake-up satellites of Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2, respectively. The two shake-up satellite peaks located at approximately 5eV above the main peaks, which is characteristic of a Co3O4 phase [59,60]. Besides, the shake-up satellite peaks are at 786.7 and 804.7eV, confirming the existence of Co3+ and Co2+ in the Co3O4@N-rGO composite, respectively. Furthermore, the Fig. 3f (Pie chart) shows the chemical composition of Co3O4@N-rGO composite and the corresponding atomic percentage of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and cobalt is approximately 41%, 4%, 33% and 22%, respectively. The XPS results strongly suggested that the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite is composed of oxygen bridged Co3O4 NPs and carbon bridged N-rGO.

The surface morphology and elemental composition of the synthesized materials were revealed by FESEM coupled with EDX elemental mapping analysis. The FESEM images of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs ( Fig. 4a-c) shows well defined small particle-like morphology with a high degree of aggregation. The synthesized Co3O4 NPs exhibits an average particle size of ∼25±5nm which is in good agreement with that of crystallite size obtained from the XRD results. Elemental mapping (Fig. 4d and e) shows that cobalt (Co) and oxygen (O) are alone present in the synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs without any contamination, which was further confirmed by EDX spectrum (Fig. 4f). Shown in Fig. S2(a-c) are the typical FESEM images of synthesized pristine GO which clearly seen that GO demonstrates a bunch of thin flexible sheet-like structure [61]. The corresponding elemental mapping (Fig. S2d and e) with EDX spectrum (Fig. S2f) revealed the elemental composition of C and O present in the synthesized GO. Apart from C and O peak, the prominent peak observed around 2eV in the EDX spectrum which corresponds to the Si originated from Si-wafer substrate. Fig. 5 shows the FESEM images with EDX elemental mapping of synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite. Graphene sheets trapped with Co3O4 NPs are clearly seen in Fig. 5a-d. Elemental mapping (Fig. 5e-h) and EDX spectrum (Fig. 5i) confirms the chemical composition of C, Co, O and N were present in the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite. In addition, these elemental maps indicate that the Co and N are homogeneously dispersed on the rGO layers, which supports the corresponding FESEM images (Fig. 5d).

HRTEM images were taken in order to investigate the morphology and structure of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite. Fig. 6a-e shows the TEM images of Co3O4@N-rGO composite with different magnifications, which can be seen that hierarchical Co3O4 NPs with a uniform size are well dispersed on the thin graphene layers. The smaller Co3O4 NPs were congregated and made hierarchical Co3O4 NPs during the hydrothermal process which is evident from FESEM image of bare Co3O4 NPs (Fig. 4) to TEM images of Co3O4@N-rGO composite. The lattice fringes of Co3O4 NPs were vividly seen in HRTEM images (Fig. 6d and e) which clearly demonstrates the well-textured nature of Co3O4 NPs. The interlayer distance of the Co3O4 NPs on the surface of the N-rGO layers is around 0.21nm, calculated from the HRTEM image (Fig. 6e). The corresponding SAED pattern (Fig. 6f) shows sharp spots due to the high degree of crystallinity of Co3O4 NPs on the surface of N-rGO sheets. In addition, SAED patterns are good agreement with XRD patterns of Co3O4 NPs.

The surface of the bare Ni-foam and modified Ni-foam (Co3O4@N-rGO on Ni-foam) was investigated by FESEM analysis which is shown in Fig. S3. As depicted in Fig S3a, bare Ni-foam exhibits a three-dimensional (3D) porous structure, which facilitates the deep penetration of the electrolyte during charge–discharge cycling. The smooth surface of bare Ni-foam is clear from high magnification FESEM images (Fig. S3b and c). As shown in Fig S3d-f, the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite are finely coated on the surface of 3D Ni-foam vividly seen in the low-magnification FESEM image. The smooth surface of bare 3D Ni-foam became rough after coating of Co3O4@N-rGO composite. The Co3O4 NPs are consistently dispersed between graphene layers, which prevents graphene layer from agglomeration effectively clear from the high-magnification FESEM image (Fig. S3f and Fig. 7a). The corresponding elemental mapping (Fig. 7b-e) shows the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composites contains C, Co, O and N elements. The Co3O4 NPs are homogeneously dispersed over the graphene sheets vividly seen in elemental mapping. Further, the elemental compositions of C, Co, O, and N were present in the Co3O4@N-rGO composites confirmed by EDX spectral analysis (Fig. 7f). The absence of other peaks (elements) in the EDX spectrum reveals the purity of synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite. Effective contact on the surface of the Ni-foam (electrode), accelerate ion and fast electron transfer in the interface between the electrolyte and Co3O4@N-rGO composite during the electrochemical process, resulting in the excellent electrochemical performance [62,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of synthesized bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performances of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite were evaluated and the results are shown in Fig. 8. The typical CV measurements of the electrodes have been carried out at various scan rates from 5 to 200mVs−1 in a potential window between −0.1 and 0.4V vs. Ag/AgCl. The area of the CV curves are increases with increasing the scan rate from 5 to 200mVs−1 but the shape of the CV curves retains the same as 5mVs−1 which reveals the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the electrodes. Furthermore, the total current density increased with increasing scan rate due to the decrements of effective interaction between the ions and electrode, which highlights the excellent capacitive behavior of the synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrode material (Fig. 8a) [64,65]. The well-defined redox peaks in the CV curve are mainly attributed to the faradaic redox reaction. At low scan rate, the faradaic redox reaction is dependent on the insertion-deinsertion of protons in the electrolyte [66]. Fig. 8b shows the GCD curves of Co3O4@N-rGO composite at different current densities ranges from 1 to 10Ag−1 in a potential window between 0 and 0.4V vs. Ag/AgCl. According to Eq. (1), the Cs of Co3O4@N-rGO composite is calculated to be 450, 375, 330, 315, 300 and 288Fg−1 at a current density of 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10Ag−1, respectively (Fig. 8c). This observation revealed the excellent capacitance retention of about 64% upon increasing the current density from 1 to 10Ag−1. The ED and PD of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite dependent on the various current densities are illustrated in Fig. 8d and e. The ED and PD were calculated using GCD curves based on the Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively. As the GCD current density increases from 1 to 10Ag−1, the energy density of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite on the Ni-foam gradually decreases from 10.0 to 6.4Whkg−1, whereas the corresponding PD gradually increases from 0.2 to 2.0kWkg−1. Fig. 8f shows the cycling performance of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrode at a current density of 4Ag−1, confirming the stable electrochemical behavior of the electrode. The percentage of Cs gradually increased from 1st to 500th cycle, around 25% of the initial capacitance in the first cycle. This interesting observation is attributed to very slow activation of the Co3O4 NPs during the first 500 cycles. Only a fraction of the electrode material was active during the initial few hundred cycles, while the other part was not activated. As the electrolyte gradually penetrated into the N-rGO, more nanoparticles were activated and contributed to the high capacitance of the electrode. The peak was followed by a gradual decrease before reaching after 550 cycles. The capacitance showed a stable behavior in the final 200 cycles with a capacitance retention of 99%. Major reasons behind the excellent capacitance retention at high current densities are due to full encapsulation of the uniformly dispersed Co3O4 NPs within the N-rGO, which prevented the particles from detachment during cycles, and the presence of conducting networks offered by the carbon matrix with an enhanced electrical conductivity. The uniformly dispersed nanoparticles increased the surface area of the active material that was in contact with the electrolyte and actively participated in a redox reaction.

In order to find the superiority of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite, the electrochemical performances of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite were compared to the bare Co3O4 NPs electrode materials which are shown in ESI Fig. S4 and Fig. 9. The electrochemical parameters such as Cs, ED and Cs retention of the bare Co3O4 NPs was lower than that of Co3O4@N-rGO composites (see ESI Fig S4, in 2M KOH electrolyte in the same potential window). The Co3O4@N-rGO composite having a higher area of CV compared with bare Co3O4 NPs at the same scan rate of 100mVs−1. In addition, the anodic and cathodic peaks of Co3O4@N-rGO composite slightly shifted toward positive and negative potential, respectively which is clear from Fig. 9a. The Co3O4@N-rGO composite delivered a Cs of 450Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 which is the highest value compared to the bare Co3O4 NPs (252Fg−1) due to its high discharging time (Fig. 9b). The impressive capacitance of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrodes arose from N-rGO. Both electrodes exhibit outstanding long cycle life over the total cycle numbers, especially the Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrode shows cycling stability with 99% capacitance retention after 1000 cycles of GCD at a current density of 4Ag−1, which is higher than 88% capacitance retention of the bare Co3O4 NPs electrode. Therefore, the Co3O4@N-rGO composite has not only a higher Cs also provides an excellent cycling stability. Table S1 shows the comparison of Cs with different graphene based Co3O4 NPs. As can be seen, the Cs of 450Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 from the present work promisingly shows high value than those previously reported for the Cs of graphene based Co3O4 NPs.

The fundamental electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors, electrodes were further investigated by the EIS measurements. Fig. 9c shows the Nyquist plots of the bare Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrodes. The plot of Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrode is composed of a distorted semicircle in the high-frequency region and incomplete semicircle with larger diameter in the low-frequency region. The distorted semicircle corresponds to double layer capacitance and charge-transfer resistance. The incomplete semicircle with larger diameter represents the electrolytic diffusion of ions through the active materials. In addition, the Nyquist plot of the Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrode presents a more vertical line than the bare Co3O4 NPs electrode in the low-frequency region, indicating the Co3O4@N-rGO composite has a better capacitive behavior. Furthermore, the Co3O4@N-rGO composite electrode has a smaller solution resistance and charge-transfer resistance than the bare Co3O4 NPs electrode in the high-frequency region which is clear from the plot (inset Fig. 9c). Moreover, the EIS data were fitted with Z-View software using equivalent circuit model of capacitance as shown in the inset of Fig. 9d. The result strongly suggesting that the Co3O4@N-rGO composite has better electrical conducting network due to the synergistic effect of N-rGO and Co3O4 NPs. The porous structure of Co3O4@N-rGO composite with a larger specific surface area is propitious to providing more electrochemical reaction sites which might be a reason for high Cs. The N-rGO layers prevent dispersed Co3O4 NPs from aggregation and accelerate the electron transport, providing a good conduction pathway and leading to smaller charge-transfer resistance [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and structural morphology of obtained N-GDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and insight structural morphology of obtained N-GDs was revealed by HRTEM analysis. As shown in Fig. 10a-c, the obtained N-GDs are monodispersed with a nearly spherical shape. The average diameter of the obtained N-GDs is around 5nm. The HRTEM image of the Fig. 10c reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21nm, which is in accordance with carbon dots derived from graphene sheets by earlier reports [68,69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the obtained N-GDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows the UV–vis and FL spectra of obtained N-GDs in an aqueous media, confirming their remarkable optical properties. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 11a) showed the N-GDs absorbed peak around 267nm and the shoulder peak around 305nm, which was assigned to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of the carbonyl group (C˭C/C˭O) or nitrogen moiety (C–N) present over the N-GDs, respectively. The excitation and emission FL spectra of the obtained N-GDs are shown in Fig. 11b. The excitation and emission were clearly observed at 320 and 412nm, respectively. The high intense FL emission peak confirms the bright FL of resulting N-GDs. The inset photograph in Fig. 11b shows the optical image of the aqueous N-GDs irradiated by a 365nm UV-light. The bright blue FL of N-GDs was strong enough to see with the naked eye. Further, FL experiment was carried out at different excitation wavelengths, in order to estimate the maximum emission (FL) intensity. The excitation-dependent emission spectrum (Fig. 11c) shows the FL intensity increases with increasing the excitation wavelength and then decreased. An optimum excitation wavelength of 320nm was found to be maximum FL intensity at the emission wavelength of 412nm with Stokes shift of 92nm. The large Stokes shift and intensive blue FL of the N-GDs indicated their potential application in bio-imaging and metal ion detection. Noticeably, the emission wavelength gradually increases (405–441nm) with increasing the excitation wavelength (290–370nm) with variable FL intensity (Fig. 11d), which may be attributed to the various surface energy traps of the N-GDs [70]. These results indicated the potential application of the resulting N-GDs in FL imaging under physiological conditions. The quantum yield of the fluorescent N-GDs was estimated to be 7% by calibrating against quinine sulfate (excited at 320nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical sensing ability of obtained N-GDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the selectivity of the obtained N-GDs, the various metal ions were performed with N-GDs under identical conditions. Fig. 12a shows the FL intensity spectra of N-GDs (blank) and N-GDs in the presence of 500μM at various metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+). It can be seen that the tremendous decrease in FL intensity is observed for N-GDs after addition of Fe3+/ Fe2+ ions, as compared with the other metal ions. Fe3+/Fe2+ might have a high affinity with the N-GDs, which formed a complex thereby quenched the FL of the N-GDs is clear from Fig. 12b. The results strongly suggested that the obtained N-GDs had outstanding selectivity, it can be used for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+/ Fe2+ ions. Subsequently, the sensitivity of the N-GDs was investigated with different concentrations of Fe2+ in the range of 50 – 500μM. As shown in Fig. 12c, the FL intensity of N-GDs was gradually decreasing with increasing Fe2+ concentrations from 50 and 500μM at ambient condition, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to the concentration of Fe2+. At the same time, Fe2+ affinity on N-GDs increases with increasing the Fe2+ concentration, but the rate of complexation between Fe2+ and N-GDs was low at higher concentration. Hence, the selectivity investigation (F0-F)/F0 versus concentration of N-GDs shows non-linear plot indicates dynamic quenching at low concentration and static quenching at high concentration of FL in this sensor system (Fig. 12d). In this dynamic quenching was supported by the decreases in the lifetime of the major component, whereas lifetime of minor component remained constant which corresponds to static quenching process [71,72]. In order to find the accurate lower sensing limit, the sensitivity of N-GDs was examined with the concentration ranges from 10 to 50μM of Fe2+ ions (Inset Fig. 12c). A strong linear relationship was observed at the concentration ranges of 10–50μM (R2 = 0.99) is clear from the inset of Fig. 12d. The LOD was 1.0μM calculated using the Eq. (7). Similar results were observed, the sensitivity of the N-GDs was examined with different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 50 – 500μM under identical conditions as Fe2+ ions (Fig. 12e and f). The excellent kinetic was observed at the concentration range of 10–50μM (R2 = 0.99) with LOD of 1.1μM. The excellent fluorescent N-GDs is an ideal fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions [73]. The resulting fluorescent N-GDs show the selective detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions from low to very high concentration. Thus, the synthesized N-GDs exhibits a wide range of detection skill which might use for the selective quenching of Fe2+/Fe3+ from the industrial waste. Moreover, the developed method is superior to the sensing systems based on organic fluorophores due to its ease and relatively inexpensive.
(7)
L O D =
3 σ
S l o p e
where, σ is the standard deviation of the signals (n = 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the label-free fluorescence quenching of N-GDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The plausible complexation between the N-GDs and Fe2+/Fe3+ ions is illustrated in Fig. 13. The surface functional groups of N-GDs induce the FL ability, such as water-soluble oxygen-containing functional groups includes hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine (nitrogen) groups. These water-soluble functional groups have a high affinity for metal ions and make complexation between them, and reduced the FL intensity. These observations indicate high sensitivity and selectivity of the present fluorescent probe toward the detection of Fe2+/Fe3+, due to stronger affinity of Fe2+/Fe3+ than other metal ions toward the hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups on the surface of the N-GDs. Hence, the abundant functional groups on the surface of the synthesized N-GDs easily coordinate with Fe2+/Fe3+ which is destroyed the FL properties of N-GDs is clear from the Fig. 13 and Eqs. (8) and (9) [74,75].
(8) 4GD-OH/GD-NH2 + Fe2+ → [Fe(OGD)4]2-/[Fe(NHGD)4]2- + 4H+
(9) 6GD-OH/GD-NH2 + Fe3+ → [Fe(OGD)6]3-/[Fe(NHGD)6]3- + 6H+

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological activities of the obtained N-GDs

ce:section.ce:para: The FL microscopy analysis was examined using N-GDs treated C. albicans and C. neoformans and untreated C. albicans and C. neoformans yeast strains. The C. albicans cells treated with 10µL of N-GDs for 2h exhibited intense FL at an excitation wavelength of 286 (deep UV) and 408nm (blue), and this FL remained stable over a period of 6h as the majority of the cell population retained FL ( Fig. 14). In the case of C. neoformans, the FL intensity was slightly diminished compared to C. albicans (Fig. S5). Thus, the both C. albicans and C. neoformans yeast cells failed to show any FL in the green (488nm) and red (561nm) laser zones [see the Electronic Supplementary information (ESI), Fig. S5 and Fig. S6]. The untreated cells (control/blank) of both of the C. albicans and C. neoformans yeast strains have no auto-FL. Further observations clearly indicated that the N-GDs emits strong FL in the cytosol, showing a blue color upon excitation at 488nm, suggesting that N-GDs were internalized in the cytoplasm via endocytosis. As the nucleus in yeast strains remains towards the polar ends of the cell, slightly increased FL towards one end of the cell in both C. albicans and C. neoformans suggested that N-GDs may have interacted with the nuclear material. The N-GDs were hydrophilic in nature and exhibited the various functional groups includes amine, carboxylic and carbonyl that aid in effective engulfment into cellular interiors, and irreversible interaction with the nuclear material. Though, the N-GDs did not exhibit any FL in the case of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (data not shown). The selective fungal staining ability of the N-GDs can be explained in terms of the differences in the cellular uptake of small molecules between bacteria and fungi.

Time growth kinetics of C. albicans in the presence of N-GDs are shown in Fig. 15a. In the growth inhibition assay, the absence of growth inhibition up to 24h incubation of the C. albicans yeast strain grown in the presence of different concentrations of N-GDs was observed. The growth curves of the yeast strain in the presence of N-GDs were similar to the growth of control sample. However, N-GDs at a concentration of 100µL slightly reduced the stationary phase of growth culture and resulted in an overall faintly reduced rate of cell growth. The absence of cell growth observed after 10h incubation time, hence, less than 10h incubation is enough to grow the yeast cells. Besides, only 10µL of the N-GDs was used for bio-imaging purposes, suggesting that small amount of N-GDs can be utilized as potent in vitro fluorescent probes for detection of fungal infections in biological samples.

Prior to considering the utility of the N-CDs as fluorescent probes for bio-imaging, it is fascinating to explore their toxicity towards C. albicans yeast cells. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of the N-CDs was further tested by a cell viability assay (respiratory activity or redox activity). Fig. 15b shows the respiratory activity of C. albicans in the presence of N-GDs with different concentrations. The respiratory activity of C. albicans in the presence of different concentrations of the test agent similar to the untreated samples (control/blank). The cellular redox activity increased with increasing the incubation time from 0 to 8h in all the concentration similar that of a control sample, which indicates the N-GDs did not induce the death of C. albicans cell. The sodium azide as a PC in the cell cytotoxicity was studied using N-GDs was to quantitatively relate the percentage inhibition in the cell viability caused by N-GDs and to compare it with a known standard cytotoxic agent (sodium azide). Sodium azide is known to inhibit the respiratory activity of mammalian cells by irreversibly binding to the terminal oxidases of electron transport chain and thereby reduce the metabolic activity in cell lines as well as in bacteria. The PC at 50µM significantly inhibited redox activity after 6h of incubation. The N-GDs did not exhibit any cytotoxicity towards the cells and were tolerable even at high concentrations which evidence that the N-GDs are cytocompatible and do not induce the C. albicans cell death. Similar results were observed for other test strains (C. neoformans yeast strain, data not shown). Moreover, these results suggest that the obtained N-GDs can be used for bio-imaging purposes due to their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

The dual products such as hierarchical Co3O4@N-rGO composites and spherical N-GDs have been successfully synthesized by a one-pot simple and efficient hydrothermal method. The Co3O4 NPs were dispersed homogeneously over the N-rGO layers revealed by FESEM with elemental mapping and HRTEM analysis. All the analytical results strongly support that the synthesized hierarchical Co3O4@N-rGO composites exhibit a high degree of crystallinity/graphitization and purity. The synthesized Co3O4@N-rGO composite was finely coated on the surface of 3D Ni-foam which delivered the high Cs of about 450 and 288Fg−1 at current densities 1 and 10Ag−1, respectively. Besides, 99% capacitance retention was observed even after 1000 cycles at a current density of 4Ag−1. The results demonstrated that the Co3O4@N-rGO composite has highest Cs with excellent rate performance and cyclic stability. This method offers a simple, economical and convenient way to obtain high-performance supercapacitor with hierarchical Co3O4@N-rGO composites, which could have great industrial application in energy storage devices. On the other hand, the N-GDs (byproduct) obtained from the Co3O4@N-rGO composites exhibits a near spherical and narrow size distribution with an average size of 5nm. The obtained N-GDs was used as an ideal fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions without any further chemical modification, owing to their good water solubility, bright FL, and high metal affinity. The N-GDs shows a wide range of detection ability which might serve the label-free selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions from the industrial waste. Furthermore, the N-GDs were used as a promising fluorescent probe for the bio-imaging of yeast cell includes C. albicans and C. neoformans due to the good cell permeability. The cytotoxicity experiment demonstrated that the yeast in presence of N-GDs has a good biocompatibility. Because of their high FL and good biocompatibility, N-GDs would offer great potential for a broad range of applications, including biomedical, bio-imaging and clinical applications in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (grant number 2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (grant number 2014R1A6A1031189). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ceramint.2017.11.034.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supercapacitive properties of hydrothermally synthesized Co3O4 nanostructures
Ultralayered Co3O4 for high-performance supercapacitor applications
Two-dimensional Co3O4 thin sheets assembled by 3D interconnected nanoflake array framework structures with enhanced supercapacitors performance derived from coordination complexes
Hollow fluffy Co3O4 cages as efficient electroactive materials for supercapacitors and oxygen evolution reaction
Effect of microwave on the nanowire morphology, optical, magnetic, and pseudocapacitance behavior of Co3O4
Fabrication of nanosheets Co3O4 by oxidation-assisted dealloying method for high capacity supercapacitors
Embedded Ag quantum dots into interconnected Co3O4 nanosheets grown on 3D graphene networks for high stable and flexible supercapacitors
Facile synthesis ultrathin mesoporous Co3O4 nanosheets for high-energy asymmetric supercapacitors
Biomorphic synthesis of mesoporous Co3O4 microtubules and their pseudocapacitive performance
Morphology controlled synthesis of nanoporous Co3O4 nanostructures and their charge storage characteristics in supercapacitors
All-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor based on Co3O4 nanoparticles on vertically aligned graphene
Morphology-controllable synthesis of cobalt oxalates and their conversion to mesoporous Co3O4 nanostructures for application in supercapacitors
Facile synthesis of Co3O4 with different morphologies loaded on amine modified graphene and their application in supercapacitors
Thin Co3O4 nanosheet array on 3D porous graphene/nickel foam as a binder-free electrode for high-performance supercapacitors
Carbon-encapsulated Co3O4@CoO@Co nanocomposites for multifunctional applications in enhanced long-life lithium storage, supercapacitor and oxygen evolution reaction
Layer-dependent growth of two-dimensional Co3O4 nanostructure arrays on graphene for high performance supercapacitors
